TRANSCRIPT
Okay, this is the Secrets of Leverage and Scale part two and this all about how to
get leverage. So, in the last section I introduced you to the idea of leverage and scale and
why it's so important and how it basically allows you to grow your business without
growing the time you put into your business. So, now it's all about leverage and this is the
very core of that. How to make more - make more money, make more impact, serve more,
do better, and not continually work harder and harder and more and more and more
hours.
So, the first thing is you have to have a focus on protecting your time. This is
something that has to be front and center for you. Just every minute you're in your
business you have to be thinking about this because the world will conspire to take your
time from you. It will conspire to distract you. The first thing that you're going to be ... It's
almost like the world will, and your psyche and your psychology will say, check your
inbox, go check your email, check your email, check your social, check your inbox. And
the only thing waiting in your inbox is the world's agenda for you and you have to impose
your own agenda on you and not let the world impose its agenda on you.
So you have to protect your time. You have to be aware that there are time
vampires out there. There are gonna be expectations that people will have for you. They
might be clients, they might be prospects, they might be vendors, they might be
employees or team members. They are going to have this way that they think you should
act and that way will take time from you and you have to stand firm. Being productive is
not about getting a lot of things done. Being productive is not about working hard. You go
to the bank, and you go to deposit a check, and they don't ask you if you worked hard for
it.
I grew up in the Midwest United States and there's a really strong work ethic in the
Midwest. And so sometimes I get stuck in this loop of thinking I have to work really,
really hard and I'm just gonna outwork everyone. But that's not what being productive is.
And really when we're talking about leverage, we're talking about making your time super
productive. Being productive is about getting the right things done, doing the right things,
knowing the things that are going to make an impact, not the things that other people
think you should do.
So be really careful. Understand that there are time vampires. There are both

norms, in other words the things that people assume you should do, and then there are
people that are time vampires that will just show up in your life and just drain time and
drain energy from you. There are drama queens and drama kings and they'll just create
drama everywhere and that drama will just suck the time and energy out of you. So you
just have to refuse to play that game.
You have to remove yourself from those situations and frankly, from some of those
relationships. And maybe some of those relationships are tough to remove yourselves
from, maybe it's your parents or your kids or something like that and it makes it really
hard. But you have to continually focus on staying away from those things that take you
off your game. Because at the end of the day, well, we all have the same amount of time,
right? And you're here listening to this because you want to create more.
And again, it could be create more income, create a better lifestyle, create more
impact, help more people, serve more people. To do that, you have to guard your time
carefully. You need to ask yourself, does this, whatever you're thinking about doing, if I
take this on, if I do this, does this take more time from me? Does my time commitment
grow as this thing grows, as this project grows? Can I get leverage here? Can I design it so
that it grows without me putting in more time or is it inherently a situation where I'm
going to continually have to put in more time to make more? If so, it's probably not a
good project to take on.
Could be, if it's early on in your business and you're fighting to get started, you're
fighting to get a foothold, fighting to get traction, to get positioning, there are times where
you do have to take on things that aren't leveraged. Or they might have high strategic
value like if you work one on one with this client, do they then end up emailing their list
about how awesome you are and put you in front of 20,000 followers or something like
that? So there are times where maybe you make a short term decision to do something
that's unleveraged because strategically, it will give you leverage in the long run. So, ask
yourself, if I do this one time, will it continue to pay dividends into the future? Dividends
in your money or exposure or positioning or learning, in time, in money, in opportunities.
If I do this one time, will it continue to pay dividends into the future?
One of the things, one of the greatest ways to get leverage, is with publishing.
Publishing, that's ... People ask me what I do and I say, I'm a publisher, I'm in publishing.
That's what my business is and that's it's been since I started out in 1996 is publishing.
And publishing's one of the best ways to get leverage because it's basically, publishing is
this idea of taking your thoughts, your ideas, your words, your voice, your writing, and

putting it out into the world so that a lot of people can see it. It's the opposite of a one to
one conversation. One to one conversations are fantastic but you're having it with one

So this is one of the best ways to get leverage. It's great to get leverage in your
marketing. It's great to get leverage in bringing new traffic and putting you in front of a
lot of people, running a lot of people through your message. It's a great way to attract

product, I mentioned this in the last section ... my Product Launch Formula. I created a
product, I published that product. I can sell it to 10 people or 100 people or 1,000 people
or 10,000 people and I don't need to put more effort into it. When I published my book, it

turning it into an audio and turning it into a podcast and turning it into a YouTube video
and a Facebook video and then having it transcribed and having it turned into a blog post,
that's inherent leverage. That's another form of leverage within your publishing.
Now, when you publish there's lots of different platforms and the platforms keep
on expanding. The choice in platforms just keeps on expanding. Now I'm a huge fan of
email. I think email is still king. It's a fantastic ... it needs to be part of your publishing. It
might not be the only part but it's the part that you control the most. So other ways to
publish, you might be social media platforms and they can be great to publish but you
don't own those platforms. So what you always want to do is focus on that email list and
building that email list. And then using that email list to drive people to your where ever
you're publishing, whatever you're publishing, however you're publishing, the email is the
hub that then sends traffic out into all those areas. It's the traffic you can control is the
inherent traffic that's built in your email list.
So if you have 10,000 people on your list, at any time you could send an email and
tell those 10,000 to go watch this video, to go listen to this podcast, go read this blog post,
go look at these photos, whatever. Now when you push that send button, do 10,000
people go? No, maybe it's only 1,000 but 1,000 people is a lot, it's huge. So email is king
and it's sort of the central hub that then sends traffic out in all these different directions.
Now then once you get outside of that in those other directions, those other

platforms come up and they seem like they're the next big thing and then all of a sudden
they're gone. Vine, MySpace, they came and went, right? Right now it's Facebook and

Instagram and on and on, Youtube and so on ...
So there are certain platforms that are better for some markets and for some
business than others. If you have an older demographic then Instagram and Snapchat are
probably not the places to be, right? Maybe it's Pinterest. If you're really great on video
then you got a few choices. It could be YouTube or it could be Instagram, it could be

video, in a set.
So you have to take a look at what your capabilities are, what your interests are,
where your market is at, and then have that dictate where you want to publish. Because
there is no one medium that's right for everyone.
Now one great way to add leverage into your business is consider adding in some
continuity, some continuity sales. Continuity is a subscription sale, so you make the sale
once and people keep on paying you until they cancel. I've almost always had some form
of continuity in my business. When I started out I had newsletters about the stock
market, so people would pay me every month or every year to get access to my
newsletters. Now I have high-end coaching programs that renew on a yearly basis. I have
my high-end platinum plus mastermind group that renews on a yearly basis, lots and lots
of different ways to do continuity. It fits into all different kinds of businesses, of types of
products. Nowadays most of the software you get is on a continuity basis.
Continuity is fantastic because it sort of lets you relax because you know how much
money is coming in every month. You don't have to go out and make a whole bunch of
new sales, you just renew everyone once a month or it could be once a year. So it lets you
relax. It gives you some level of a guaranteed income. Now it does take work and if you're
the one that ... if you're creating content for it then you will continue to have that work,
that what I call mental a overhang of having to create that content. So don't just dismiss
that lightly because, boy, it can be a grind. It can wear you down.
But there are forms of continuity where you don't have to do that, like if you have a
community type of program. Or in the first section of this training I talked about this one
membership site I had that was just nothing but a user group for a piece of investing
software. Then I didn't have ... I had zero mental overhang on that. Now that doesn't say
that just because you create something where you are creating content every month or
every quarter or every year, that doesn't mean it's a horrible thing. Because it is still
highly leveraged and it can scale. It is just something that you have to keep up with. So
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

you have think that through and think about whether you're willing to do that.
Now, if you do add continuity it's great because it does let you relax, it gives you
that guaranteed income. But what you will have is, your renewal rate will become one of
the most important metrics in your business that you will end up obsessing over and
you're always gonna be fighting churn. Churn is this idea of people come and people go.
And there is a little bit, especially if you're creating content, if you're not replacing people
as quickly as they're leaving, and hopefully more than replace them and growing, then it
becomes psychologically more burdensome to create content if your income is dropping.
And another thing is, as the program grows, as it scales, then it becomes more
difficult to replace the people that leave. Just on a percentage basis, if you have 100
people and 90% of them renew every month, that means you lose 10 people a month. And
so you have to replace 10 people a month to keep your income the same. You have to
replace more than 10 people to increase your income. If you have 10,000 people in that
program, 10,000 people, and you have a 90% renewal, now you're losing 1,000 people
every month and now you have to replace 1,000 people. That's a bigger task. So, none of
this is rocket science but it's also stuff I never thought about when I first started creating a
continuity program and it's something I want you to think about.
Now, in your business there ... I'm gonna try to pronounce something here. I'm
gonna mess this up, but there's this idea of, I think it's Occam, Occam's razor, and it's this
principle of all other things being able equal, the simplest solution is preferable. This is
something, I don't know, for some reason I just naturally gravitate toward the simple
solutions and keeping my business simple. And I can tell you, there's a lot of people in my
market, a lot of people them are good friends of mine who have very similar businesses to
mine, and I also have some special insight into the background of most of those
businesses. And I'll tell you that most of them are more complex than mine. I have one of
the simplest businesses in this market. And I also know that I have one of the highest
margins in the market and I think there's a direct correlation between those. It's easy to
make things complicated and you want to avoid it in general. In general, you want to try
to keep things as simple as possible and focus on having as high a margin as possible.
Now another thing that will give you leverage is having an evergreen marketing
system so that ... And I tell you this because I know a lot of the people listening to this will
be people that have gone through or are involved in my Product Launch Formula
training. And Product Launch Formula has become sort of the de facto gold standard for
conversions in our market. I mean, it just generates fantastic results and a lot of people

end up building a business purely around episodic launches. And there's an inherent
danger in that because sometimes launches don't go fantastic. Things can come up,
hurricanes happen, terrorist attacks happen, technical outages happen, server crashes
happen, so there is some risk in just depending on, say, one launch a year. And creating
an evergreen system to continually drive sales is just a way to add in a level of safety into
your business. So I would highly suggest looking at creating evergreen systems in your
marketing.
Another way to get leverage is to reuse your marketing. So this I had learned early
on. I used to, back in my stock market days when I was publishing about the stock
market, we published every single market day of the year, every single trading day of the
year. Most years there's 252 trading days. And I had this system where people could
watch a portion of what we were doing on a free trial basis. So they could basically sample
of our material and it would come out just a little bit late for them. The utility was greatly
diminished but they could still watch what we were saying was gonna happen in the stock
market. They could do it every single day and they could do it as long as they wanted.
One time I sat down to do an analysis of how long people were on my list before
they ended up buying. What I noticed is of the people that bought, about half of them
would buy in the first week and about another 25% would buy in the first 30 days. So 75%
of the people that would buy from me would buy within the 30 days but the other 25%
would be spread out over the next several months and even longer. And then I found
someone who identified, who'd actually been on my email list for more than four years
before he purchased. In other words, he'd gotten more than 1,000 emails from me until it
was time for him to buy. Either he was eventually convinced of the value or it was just the
right time in his life or it was affordable for him or whatever, I don't know why but after
four years he decided to buy.
And that's what drove in ... I mean, it was just a huge lesson in this idea of don't get
sick of your marketing, keep your marketing going, and this whole idea of marketing
stamina, having a stamina in your marketing to keep on going. Most people get sick of
their own marketing before the market does so have a continual process to just nonstop
be marketing to your prospects.
And feel free to reuse emails, to reuse marketing campaigns, to reuse webinars, to
reuse your launches. Don't get sick of your marketing because in this business, certainly
creating product and serving your customers is one of the big things you need to do but
also creating your marketing is a big task. When you can create marketing that lives on,

that you can reuse over and over and over, that's where you get leverage. That's a really
big leverage point. It's creating marketing that will continue to work as evergreen, not a
one-time deal.
That doesn't mean you can't do one-time campaigns, that you can't take advantage
of something that changes in your market, of an event that comes up, of an outside event.
You can, feel free to take care of those but always in your marketing, ask yourself, how can
I create this so this marketing can be used over and over and over? Whether it's videos or
sales letters or sales videos or emails or whatever, how can I build this so I can use it over
and over and over?
